line_devices.quotation: What thou dost foist upon us that is old
line_devices.explanation: Time as confidence trickster, fraudulently presenting old things as new.

line_devices.quotation: Thy registers and thee I both defy
line_devices.explanation: The defiance extends from 'thy' (possessive) to 'thee' (personal) -- both Time's evidence and Time itself rejected.

line_devices.quotation: despite thy scythe and thee
line_devices.explanation: The scythe as Time's/Death's traditional weapon -- the Speaker vows to endure despite the instrument of destruction.

modes.rationale: The sonnet is a sustained argument against Time -- denial, evidence, rejection of counter-evidence, vow.
The defiance of Time is one of the sequence's master themes, here given its most direct and monumental expression.
The apostrophe to Time creates a mythological confrontation scene.
The heavily stressed monosyllables of the couplet ('This I do vow') give the vow its lapidary weight.

analysis.argument_summary: The Speaker argues that his love is not a 'child of state' (product of political circumstance) but was built 'far from accident.' It is immune to fashion, policy, and the heresy of short-term thinking. The couplet calls as witnesses 'the fools of time, which die for goodness, who have lived for crime.'
analysis.emotional_arc: hypothetical political love -> denial -> positive characterization of true love -> enigmatic witnesses
analysis.dramatic_situation: The most politically dense sonnet in the sequence. The Speaker distinguishes his love from the transactional affections of courtly life, building an argument for love as a permanent structure immune to political weather.
analysis.subtext: The 'fools of time, which die for goodness, who have lived for crime' is one of the most debated couplets in Shakespeare. It may refer to political martyrs (Essex?), religious converts on the scaffold, or any hypocrites whose deathbed repentance follows a life of self-interest.
analysis.analysis_text: Sonnet 124 is the sequence's most politically sophisticated poem, conducting its love argument through the vocabulary of statecraft, architecture, and heresy. The opening conditional establishes the hypothetical: 'If my dear love were but the child of state, / It might for Fortune's bastard be unfather'd.' If his love were merely a product of political circumstance ('child of state'), it could be disowned ('unfather'd') as Fortune's illegitimate offspring. The architectural imagery of the second quatrain builds the counter-claim: 'No, it was builded far from accident.' This love was not thrown up by circumstance but deliberately constructed at a remove from contingency. 'It suffers not in smiling pomp, nor falls / Under the blow of thralled discontent' -- it is immune to both prosperity (smiling pomp) and oppression (thralled discontent). The language is political: 'pomp' and 'discontent' are the twin poles of court life. Line 8 is topical: 'Whereto th' inviting time our fashion calls' -- the current political moment seduces people into fashionable discontent. The third quatrain introduces the most complex imagery: 'It fears not policy, that heretic.' Policy (political calculation) is called a heretic -- someone who holds false doctrine. Policy 'works on leases of short-number'd hours' -- it operates on short-term contracts, whereas true love is permanent. 'But all alone stands hugely politic' -- love is 'politic' (wise, strategic) precisely by refusing to be political. The paradox is that true political wisdom consists in transcending politics. The couplet summons its enigmatic witnesses: 'the fools of time, / Which die for goodness, who have lived for crime.' These are people whose lives were criminal but whose deaths were virtuous -- deathbed converts, political martyrs, or the religiously executed. They witness to the distinction between temporal calculation and eternal commitment by embodying its failure.

directing.scene_setting: The most abstract and politically charged sonnet in the group. The Speaker is not addressing the FYM personally but making a philosophical declaration about the nature of love in a political world.
directing.character_note: The Speaker speaks as a political philosopher, not a lover. The vocabulary of statecraft, heresy, and law replaces the vocabulary of emotion.
directing.fym_note: The FYM is implied as the object of the 'dear love' but is not addressed. The argument is conducted over his head, against the world.
directing.overall_arc: Establish the political hypothetical in Q1, build the architectural counter-claim in Q2, develop the heresy metaphor in Q3, and deliver the enigmatic couplet.

key_moments.note: 'If my dear love were but the child of state' -- the conditional is already a denial. The Speaker is ruling out the political reading.
'It was builded far from accident' -- the positive claim. Love as deliberate construction, not chance product.
'Policy, that heretic' -- policy as false doctrine. A charged word in Reformation England.
'The fools of time' -- the enigmatic witnesses. Their meaning has been debated for four centuries.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The conditional should be clearly conditional -- 'If' sets up a possibility that will be denied.
line_annotations.gloss: If my precious love were merely a product of political circumstance
line_annotations.commentary: 'Child of state' -- born from political conditions, contingent on power relations. The metaphor of love as offspring of the state frames the entire political argument.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Bastard' and 'unfather'd' carry legal weight -- illegitimate, disowned.
line_annotations.gloss: It could be disowned as Fortune's illegitimate child
line_annotations.commentary: 'Fortune's bastard' -- illegitimate offspring of chance. 'Unfather'd' -- denied paternity, disowned. The legal language makes love-as-child-of-state a matter of inheritance law.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Builded far from accident' -- emphasis on deliberate construction. This love was engineered, not accidental.
line_annotations.gloss: No, it was constructed far from any contingency
line_annotations.commentary: 'Builded' rather than 'built' -- the archaic form gives weight and permanence. 'Far from accident' -- at a remove from all contingency and chance.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Thralled discontent' -- enslaved dissatisfaction. A phrase heavy with political resonance.
line_annotations.gloss: Nor falls under the blow of enslaved discontentment
line_annotations.commentary: 'Thralled discontent' -- discontent that is itself enslaved, perhaps to faction or ideology. The phrase compresses a political analysis into two words.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'That heretic' -- policy is named as a heretic. In Reformation England, this is a capital accusation.
line_annotations.gloss: It does not fear political calculation, that heretic
line_annotations.commentary: 'Policy, that heretic' -- political calculation as false doctrine. The identification of pragmatism with heresy is a profound moral claim: short-term thinking is spiritual error.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Short-number'd hours' -- the contempt for short-term thinking should be audible.
line_annotations.gloss: Which operates on short-term contracts of limited duration
line_annotations.commentary: 'Leases of short-number'd hours' -- policy works on temporary contracts, unlike love's permanent freehold. The legal/property metaphor distinguishes leasehold (temporary) from freehold (permanent).

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Hugely politic' -- the paradox should land: true political wisdom is to transcend politics.
line_annotations.gloss: But stands alone, massively wise in its own way
line_annotations.commentary: The paradox: love is 'politic' (politically wise) precisely by refusing to be political. Standing 'all alone' is its strategy -- isolation as strength.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'The fools of time' -- these witnesses are ambiguous figures. Deliver with the weight of an enigma.
line_annotations.gloss: As witnesses I call the fools of time
line_annotations.commentary: 'Fools of time' -- those who were fooled by temporal calculation, or those whom time made fools of. The phrase is deliberately enigmatic.

character_appearances.evidence: 'My dear love' -- the Speaker as political philosopher of love
The 'dear love' is directed at the FYM, though he is not addressed directly

modes.rationale: The political philosophy of love -- its immunity to state, policy, and fashion -- is the sonnet's primary concern.
The conditional-then-denial structure, building through definition to witness-calling, is a formal argument.
The political vocabulary (child of state, thralled discontent, heretic, policy) is deeply embedded in Jacobean politics.
Penultimate sonnet in the FYM sequence proper, building toward 125's climactic envoi.

analysis.argument_summary: The Speaker asks whether outward ceremony -- bearing the canopy, laying great bases, dwelling on form -- has any value when it proves as transient as waste and ruin. He rejects all external display in favor of a simple, mutual 'oblation' of self. The couplet dismisses a 'suborned informer,' declaring that a true soul stands least in its control when most impeached.
analysis.emotional_arc: rhetorical question about ceremony -> examples of external failure -> rejection in favor of inward devotion -> defiance of the informer
analysis.dramatic_situation: The final sonnet of the FYM sequence proper (before 126's envoi). The Speaker rejects all the external trappings of patronage and display in favor of a stripped-down, mutual devotion. The mysterious 'suborned informer' of the couplet introduces a note of paranoia or political danger.
analysis.subtext: Bearing the canopy was a specific honor at court ceremonies -- the Speaker may be rejecting courtly patronage itself. The 'suborned informer' could be a real person (a rival, a gossip, a political spy) or an allegorical figure (Flattery, Time, the World). The sonnet reads as a farewell to everything except essential love.
analysis.analysis_text: Sonnet 125 is the climactic envoi of the FYM sequence, a poem of radical renunciation that strips away every form of external display to arrive at the essence of devotion. The opening question is rhetorical and self-answering: 'Were't aught to me I bore the canopy, / With my extern the outward honouring?' Would it matter if the Speaker participated in the external ceremonies of patronage -- bearing the ceremonial canopy over a dignitary, honoring outward forms with his outward self? The 'extern' (external self) is deliberately separated from the inner truth. Lines 3-4 extend the question to monumental ambition: 'Or laid great bases for eternity, / Which proves more short than waste or ruining?' Building foundations for eternal structures that prove shorter-lived than the decay they were meant to resist -- the irony of permanence projects that cannot outlast entropy. The second quatrain catalogs the failures of externalism: 'dwellers on form and favour' who 'Lose all and more by paying too much rent / For compound sweet.' Those who invest in appearances pay excessive rent for a sweetness that is 'compound' (complex, artificial) rather than 'simple' (pure, uncompounded). They are 'Pitiful thrivers, in their gazing spent' -- people who succeed only in exhausting themselves through looking. At line 9, the volta rejects all this with lapidary simplicity: 'No; let me be obsequious in thy heart.' 'Obsequious' means dutifully attentive (not its modern pejorative sense), and the location is crucial -- 'in thy heart,' not in the world's view. 'Take thou my oblation, poor but free' -- the offering is humble but unencumbered, 'not mix'd with seconds' (inferior additions), knowing 'no art' (no artifice). 'But mutual render, only me for thee' -- the simplest possible exchange, stripped of all ornamentation. The couplet introduces the enigmatic 'suborned informer' -- someone bribed to bear false witness. 'Hence, thou suborned informer!' is a dramatic expulsion: the spy, the gossip, the false accuser is banished. 'A true soul / When most impeach'd, stands least in thy control' -- the truest soul is most immune to accusation precisely when most accused. This is the sequence's final word on the relationship between truth and reputation, and it is characteristically paradoxical.

directing.scene_setting: The final statement of the FYM sequence. The Speaker has shed every disguise, every ceremony, every rhetorical flourish. What remains is the simplest possible offering: me for thee.
directing.character_note: The Speaker at his most purified and essential. The wit, the rhetoric, the elaborate conceits of earlier sonnets have been burned away. What speaks here is the residue after all the dross has been consumed.
directing.fym_note: The FYM is asked to accept the oblation -- a religious term for a sacrificial offering. The relationship is now liturgical, not courtly.
directing.dl_note: Absent. The 'suborned informer' may be a real person who has tried to damage the Speaker-FYM relationship.
directing.overall_arc: Open with the rhetorical questions about ceremony, catalog the failures of externalism, make the stripped-down offering at line 9, and expel the informer in the couplet.

key_moments.note: 'Were't aught to me I bore the canopy' -- the rhetorical question expects 'no.' The Speaker is already past ceremony.
'Dwellers on form and favour' -- the warning example. Others who invested in appearances and lost everything.
'No; let me be obsequious in thy heart' -- the volta. Everything external is rejected. Only the heart matters.
'My oblation, poor but free' -- the central offering. 'Poor but free' is the Speaker's final self-characterization.
'Hence, thou suborned informer!' -- the dramatic expulsion. A threat ejected from the sacred space of mutual devotion.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The question expects 'no.' The Speaker has already decided these ceremonies are worthless.
line_annotations.gloss: Would it matter to me that I carried the ceremonial canopy?
line_annotations.commentary: Bearing the canopy was a specific honor at court processions. The Speaker asks whether this external privilege has any real value.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Great bases for eternity' -- speak with the ironic awareness that such ambitions always fail.
line_annotations.gloss: Or laid massive foundations intended to last forever
line_annotations.commentary: 'Great bases' -- architectural foundations for monumental structures. The ambition of building for eternity is immediately undercut by line 4's revelation of failure.

line_annotations.delivery_note: The punchline of the question: eternal foundations prove shorter-lived than decay itself.
line_annotations.gloss: Which turns out to be shorter-lived than decay or ruin
line_annotations.commentary: The paradox: the eternity-project proves 'more short than waste' -- even the waste outlasts the structure. Ambition defeated by entropy.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Lose all and more' -- the total loss of those who invested in appearances. Economic metaphor: excessive rent for a leasehold pleasure.
line_annotations.gloss: Lose everything and more by paying too much for their lease on appearance
line_annotations.commentary: 'Paying too much rent' -- the economic metaphor from 124 returns. Form and favour are leasehold pleasures requiring constant payment.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Compound sweet' versus 'simple savour' -- artificial complexity versus natural plainness. The preference is clear.
line_annotations.gloss: For a complex artificial sweetness, abandoning simple natural flavor
line_annotations.commentary: 'Compound' (mixed, artificial) versus 'simple' (pure, uncompounded) -- an alchemical/pharmaceutical distinction. Simple savour is the genuine article; compound sweet is adulterated.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'No' rejects everything that preceded it. 'In thy heart' -- the only location that matters. Speak with the simplicity of arrival.
line_annotations.gloss: No -- let me be devoted within your heart
line_annotations.commentary: 'Obsequious' means dutifully devoted (not sycophantic). The location 'in thy heart' is the only space the Speaker claims -- not in the world's view, not in ceremony, but in the beloved's interior.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Oblation' is a religious offering -- a sacrifice. 'Poor but free' is the Speaker's final self-description.
line_annotations.gloss: And accept my offering, humble but unencumbered
line_annotations.commentary: 'Oblation' elevates the offering to the liturgical -- this is a sacramental act, not a transaction. 'Poor but free' -- the offering's poverty is its value, because it comes without strings.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Only me for thee' -- the simplest possible exchange. Four monosyllables. Let them be enough.
line_annotations.gloss: But a mutual exchange: only me for you
line_annotations.commentary: 'Mutual render' -- reciprocal surrender. 'Only me for thee' is the sequence's most essential statement: all rhetoric, all ceremony, all conceit reduced to four words.

line_annotations.delivery_note: 'Hence!' is a command of banishment. The 'suborned informer' is violently ejected from the sacred space.
line_annotations.gloss: Away with you, bribed false witness!
line_annotations.commentary: 'Suborned informer' -- someone bribed to give false testimony. The dramatic expulsion disrupts the sonnet's devotional calm with a sudden flash of political danger.

character_appearances.evidence: 'In thy heart,' 'take thou my oblation,' 'only me for thee' -- the FYM as recipient of the oblation
'My extern,' 'my oblation,' 'a true soul' -- the Speaker as purified offerer
The 'suborned informer' may be a rival or enemy who has tried to undermine the Speaker's relationship with the FYM

modes.rationale: The rejection of ceremony for substance is the sonnet's -- and the FYM sequence's -- culminating theme.
The expulsion of the suborned informer creates a dramatic rupture that gives the couplet its extraordinary force.
The structure (rhetorical questions, negative examples, positive offering, expulsion) is a carefully engineered argument.
The climactic envoi of the FYM sequence -- everything the 153 preceding sonnets have built toward is distilled here.
